key_insight: In neutron-irradiated tungsten, strain rate and deformation length scale affect nanoindentation response through different mechanisms: strain rate mainly changes thermally activated flow stress, while indentation depth controls plastic-zone size, pile-up, and GND buildup.
engineering_relevance: A calibrated strain-gradient crystal plasticity model can predict irradiation-hardened tungsten behavior across strain rates and loading geometries, supporting design for transient, high-radiation service conditions.
method: Spherical nanoindentation on unirradiated and neutron-irradiated single-crystal tungsten over 3.2e-5 to 3.2e-3 s^-1
AFM and HR-EBSD measured pile-up, residual lattice strain, and GND distributions
CPFE model coupled thermally activated slip, GND hardening, irradiation obstacle hardening, and strain-dependent softening
evidence_signal: A single calibrated experimental condition was sufficient for the model to validate across all other strain rates and to reproduce independent depth and polycrystal compression trends.
watch_item: Transferability is promising, but the model still depends on accurate calibration of irradiation hardening and gradient parameters, which may vary with dose, temperature, and microstructure.
paper_title: Decoupling Strain-Rate Sensitivity and Deformation Length Scale Effects in Neutron-Irradiated Tungsten: A Coupled Nano-Indentation, HR-EBSD and Crystal Plasticity Study
